Facts.text: Rome captures the first wall of the city of Jerusalem
Battle of the Kalka River: Mongol force led by Jebei and Subedei defeat a Russian alliance, after feigning their retreat, leading to the death of 20,000 Russians
Jacoba of Bavaria becomes countess of Holland/Zealand/Henegouwen
Martin Frobisher sails from Harwich, England, to Frobisher Bay, Canada. Eventually mines fools gold, famously used to pave the streets of London.
The colony of Massachusetts Bay annexes Maine colony
Swedish forces take Dutch-held fort of Fort Casimir on the Delaware River without resistance after the fort runs out of gunpowder
Netherlands, England and France sign Treaty of The Hague
Jerusalem's rabbi Sjabtai Tswi proclaims himself Messiah
John Salomonsz elected chief of Saint-Eustatius
France, Britain & Netherlands sign accord of Paris
French troops conquer Kortrijk
The Province of Pennsylvania bans all theater productions
Alferez Manuel Quimper explores the Strait of Juan de Fuca (now the international border between Canada and the US)
US copyright law enacted
In Australia, Lawson, Blaxland and Wentworth reach Mount Blaxland, marking the end of a route across the Blue Mountains
Cathedral of Assumption of Blessed Virgin Mary, 1st US Catholic cathedral, is dedicated in Baltimore, designed by Benjamin Henry Latrobe
HMS Beagle anchors in Simons Bay, Cape of Good Hope
Astor Hotel opens in NYC, it later becomes the Waldorf-Astoria
Rotterdam-Hague Railway opens
Last edition of Orange sheet "Journal de La Haye"
Elisha Kane's Arctic expedition leaves NY aboard Advance
Philadelphia A's organize to play "town ball" became baseball 20 years later
General Beauregard is given command of Confederate Alexandria Line
Mint at New Orleans closes
Battle of Seven Pines Virginia (Fair Oaks)
Britain's Dr. James Moore wins the first recorded bicycle race, a 2 km velocipede race at Parc de Saint-Cloud, Paris
First Memorial Day parade held in Ironton, Ohio
Congress passes 1st Enforcement Act (rights of blacks)
E. J. DeSemdt patents asphalt pavement
Reciprocity Treaty between US & Hawaii ratified
German battleship SMS Grosser Kurfürst sinks, 284 killed
US Congress accepts decrease in dollar circulation
First electric railway opens at the Berlin Trade Exposition
League of American Wheelmen forms in Newport, Rhode Island, the first US bicycle association
French fleet under Pierre begins siege of Tamatave, Madagascar
Johnstown Flood; 2,209 die in Johnston, Pennsylvania when the South Fork Dam, located on the Little Conemaugh River fails
Ulm Minster, in Ulm, Germany, the tallest church in the world with a steeple 161.5m high, is finally completed, after foundation stone laid in 1377
Work on Trans-Siberian railway begins
Whitcomb Judson of Chicago, patents a hookless fastening (zipper)
-June 5] Conference of Bloemfontein fails
Bronx acquires Keltch Memorial Park
British troops under Lord Roberts occupy Johannesburg
Piet de Law captures Lt Col BE Spragges, 13th Battalion Irish Imperial Yeomanry
Tom Hayward scores 1,000th cricket run of season (sets record 1074)
US troops arrive in Beijing, help put down Boxer Rebellion
At the opening of the Greek National Assembly, Prince George, High Commissioner of Crete, asks it to endorse the union of Crete with Greece; the proposal is later rejected
Australia Cricket all out 36 v England, Edgbaston, their lowest ever
Labor trouble and riots lead Spanish King Alfonso XII to impose martial law
Treaty of Vereeniging is signed, ending the Second Boer War and making Transvaal and the Orange Free State self-governing British colonies
Taxis first begin operating in New York City
Miss Pottelsberghe de la Pottery is 1st airplane passenger (Belgium)
Cape of Good Hope becomes part of Union of South Africa
Glenn Curtiss flies from Albany to NYC
Union of South Africa declares independence from the United Kingdom
US marines land on Cuba
Alexis Ahlgren runs world record maraton (2:36:06.6)
US 17th amendment (direct election of senators) declared ratified
Chicago White Sox Joe Benz no-hits Cleveland Indians, 6-1
An LZ-38 Zeppelin makes an air raid on London
NC-4 aircraft commanded by AC Read completes 1st crossing of Atlantic
A large-scale race riot breaks out in Tulsa, Oklahoma, later described as the worst incident of racial violence in American history; around 150-300 African Americans killed
Boston Red Sox 1st baseman John "Stuffy" McInnis begins an errorless string of 1,700 chances
China & USSR exchange diplomats
The South African Indian Congress (SAIC) forms in Durban, South Africa, with Omar Hajee Amod Jhaveri as President
China recognizes the USSR
Kruger National Park is established as South Africa's first national park
Portuguese president Bernardino Machedo resigns after coup
Sesquicentennial Exposition opens in Philadelphia
Tiger 1st baseman Johnny Neun makes an unassisted triple play
Charlie Hallows scores his 1,000th run of Cricket season
First aerial crossing of the Pacific takes off from Oakland
Atlantic City Convention Center opens
Building begins on Albert Canal in Belgium
Comet 73P/1930 (Schwassmann-Wachmann 3) approaches 0.0617 AUs of Earth
Philadelphia Athletics's Max Bishop draws 8 walks in a doubleheader to set MLB record

The Barmen Declaration is published by a group of
church leaders in Germany including Karl Barth to help Christians withstand the challenges of the Nazi party
A 7.7 magnitude earthquake destroys Quetta in Balochistan, British India (now Pakistan), killing an estimated 40,000 people
1st quadruplets to finish college (Baylor University)
German warships bombard Almeria, Spain
Bill Edrich scores his 1,000th run of cricket season, all at Lord's
-June 1) 32.0 cm rain falls on Burlington Kansas (state record)
1st issue of "Parade" goes on sale
A Luftwaffe air raid in Dublin, in neutral Ireland, claims 38 lives
German occupiers forbid Jews access to beaches and swimming pools
Nazi occupiers forbid Jews access to beaches and swimming pools in the Netherlands
German Luftwaffe bombs Canterbury, England
U-boats sink and damage 146 allied ships this month (722,666 tons)
"Archie" comic strip 1st broadcast on radio
42 U-boats sunk by the Allies this month
Cards Mort Cooper pitches 1st of back-to-back one-hitters
Communists seize power in Hungary
Eastern DC-4 crashes between Ft Deposit & Perryville, Maryland, kills 53
Italian government of Gasperi forms
Charley Lupica begins his stay on a 4-foot square platform atop a 60-foot pole, vowing to remain until the Indians clinch the pennant (they don't, and he stays for 117 days)
Netherlands & South Africa sign cultural accord
Lebanese president Camille Shamun disbands government
WSUN TV channel 38 in St Petersburg-Tampa, FL (IND) 1st broadcast
Construction begins on Soviet cosmodrome launch facilities
Great Britain proclaims emergency crisis due to rail strike
US Supreme Court orders school integration "with all deliberate speed"
Great Britain performs nuclear test at Christmas Island (atmospheric)
Dick Dale invents "surf music" with "Let's Go Trippin"
US performs nuclear test at Bikini Island (atmospheric tests)
Arthur Michael Ramsey appointed the 100th Archbishop of Canterbury
Judge Irving Kaufman orders Board of Ed of New Rochelle, to integrate
Union of South Africa becomes a republic, leaves Commonwealth
"Tell It To Groucho" last airs on CBS-TV
The West Indies Federation, made up of former British Caribbean territories, dissolves
NAACP Youth Council begins  a series of sit-ins at segregated lunch counters in Oklahoma City, Oklahoma; nearly two dozen businesses change their policies to serve all
Charles Schmid kills first Pied Piper victim
SF Giants beat NY Mets, 8-6, in 23 innings (2nd game) (7 hrs 32 mins)
7.75 Ancash earthquake off the coast of Peru kills 66,000-70,000 people and triggers the world's deadliest avalanche
KDUB TV channel 40 in Dubuque, IA (ABC) begins broadcasting
WDXR (now WKPD) TV channel 29 in Paducah, KY (PBS) begins broadcasting
Israel and Syria sign an agreement concerning Golan Heights
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
"Fight the Power" single released by The Isley Brothers (Billboard Soul Song of the Year, 1975)
Fred Newman makes 12,874 baskets in a one-day exhibition
The Who set the record for the loudest concert of all time at 120 decibels at 50 meters at The Valley in Charlton, London
Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani becomes heir apparent to throne of Qatar
Trans-Alaska oil pipeline completed, one of the world's largest pipeline systems
Radio City Music Hall (NYC) reopens
"Love Stinks" by J. Geils Band peaks at #38
Police & youthful rebels battle in Zurich
57th National Spelling Bee: Daniel Greenblatt wins spelling luge
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Guatemala adopts constitution
Methylenedioxymethamphetamine (MDMA) became a Schedule I drug in the United States
New Orleans Saints are sold for $70,204,000
Tornado outbreak in the United States and Canada sees 41 tornadoes hit Ohio, Pennsylvania, New York and Ontario, leaving 90 dead
Saul Ballesteros drives 3 golf balls off Mt McKinley, Alaska
Stanley Cup Final, Northlands Coliseum, Edmonton, AL: Edmonton Oilers beat Philadelphia Flyers, 3-1 to win title 4 games to 3: Oilers' 3rd SC
"Rambling with Gambling" 20,000th radio program on WOR-AM (NYC)
1st International Rock Awards in the US
Speaker of US House of Representatives Jim Wright resigns
63rd National Spelling Bee: Amy Marie Dimak wins spelling fibranne
BPAA US Women's Bowling Open won by Dana Miller-Mackie
NYC's Zodiac killer shoots 3rd victim, Joseph Ponce
Sides in Angola sign a treaty ending 16 year civil war
"The 1992 Boat Show" - the 193rd and final episode of US comedy series "Night Court" - airs on NBC-TV, completing a 9 season run
46th Tony Awards: "Dancing at Lughnasa" (play) & "Crazy For You" (musical) win
5th Children's Miracle Network Telethon raises $1,060,000
The Washington Post reveals the existence of Project Greek Island, the secret doomsday bunker in Greenbrier, West Virginia
President Dobrica Ćosić of the Federal Republic of Yugoslavia flees
Padres scores 13 in 2nd vs Pirates
Mark Van Thillo and Abigail Alling, former biospherian win $100,000 lawsuit against Biospheric Development for Space Biospheres Ventures
Super Rugby Final, Eden Park, Auckland: Blues win second straight title with a 23-7 victory over ACT Brumbies; fullback Adrian Cashmore lands 3 penalties & 2 conversions for the winners
The Confederation Bridge opens, linking Prince Edward Island with mainland New Brunswick
US reality competition TV show "Survivor" hosted by Jeff Probst premieres
The New Jersey Nets defeat the Boston Celtics 96-88 in Game 6 of the NBA's Eastern Conference Championship, winning the series 4 games to 2 to advance to their first NBA Finals appearance.
British children's cartoon "Peppa Pig" created by Astley Baker Davies premieres on Channel 5
Mark Felt, former FBI high ranking official revealed as "Deep Throat" source during Watergate investigations in "Vanity Fair" article

Gaza Flotilla raid: Israeli Shayetet 13 soldiers board ships trying to break blockade of Gaze, during violent confrontation aboard MV Mavi Marmara 9 activists killed and several activists and soldiers injured
Egypt formally ends its 31 year state of emergency
First tornado chaser to be killed by a tornado; Tim Samara and two others killed by El Reno tornado in Oklahoma, the widest ever recorded at 2.6 miles (4.2 km)
Harriette Thompson aged 92 and 65 days becomes the oldest woman to complete a marathon (Suja Rock ’n’ Roll Marathon in San Diego)
Danish government bans garments that cover the face, including the niqab and burqa
Uganda's parliament imposes tax on social media to stop gossip
Disgruntled employee shoots and kills 12 people at a US municipal building in Virginia Beach, Virginia
China's ruling Communist Party announces it will allow married couples to have three children, in effort to boost falling birthrates
Peru says its COVID-19 death toll is three times higher than its official count (180,764 vs 68,000), making it one of the hardest hit countries per capita
Tulsa, Oklahoma, marks 100 year anniversary of the massacre of hundreds of black residents by a white mob in the Greenwood neighborhood
Lost 3,400 year-old Bronze Age city unearthed on the Tigris river, Iraq due to drought, likely part of the Mittani Empire, including 100 cuneiform tablets
Latvia elects its Foreign Minister Edgars Rinkevics as President - first openly gay leader of a Baltic state
Los Angeles County jury convicts American actor Danny Masterson on two of three counts of forcible rape

NASA panel investigating unidentified flying objects at its first open meeting highlights stigma involved in reporting sightings and lack of high-quality data (mysterious radio signals turned out to be a local microwave)
Trillions of periodical cicadas (Magicicada) emerge together in the US for the first time in 221 years, brood XIX (Great Southern Brood) after 13 years and brood XIII after 17 years
David Seymour (ACT) succeeds Winston Peters (NZ First) as New Zealand's Deputy Prime Minister
Ramesses II, also known as Ramesses the Great becomes Pharaoh of Ancient Egypt's 19th Dynasty

Dutch Catholic activist Engel Korsendochter leads a march of 300 women from the Guild of the Holy Sacrament protesting plans to replace a treasured chapel with a wool warehouse in Amsterdam; they are charged with rebellion, fined, and banished for 4 years - most were pardoned upon appeal to Holy Roman EmperorCharles V
Former Lord Chancellor of EnglandFrancis Baconis imprisoned for corruption in the Tower of London for 1 or 2 nights until released byKing James I
Citing poor eyesight, English civil servantSamuel Pepysrecords the last entry in his famous diary
Madison Square Garden opens in New York, named after 4th PresidentJames Madison
Dr John Harvey Kellogg of Battle Creek, Michigan files application for patent for "flaked cereal, and process of making same", controversially excluding his younger brotherWill Keith Kellogg
Remains ofJefferson Davismoved from New Orleans and re-interred at Hollywood Cemetery in Richmond, Virginia, per his widow's request
Assassination attempt on KingAlfonso XIII& Victoria of Battenberg in Madrid during the procession after their marriage in Madrid by a Catalan anarchist kills 30
National Conference on the Negro holds its first meeting in United Charities Building, New York (earlier form of the NAACP)
Former Mexican PresidentPorfirio Díazflees the country during the Mexican Revolution, and heads to Spain
RMS Titaniclaunched in Belfast
HMS Invincible explodes after taking fire during theBattle of Jutland, killing 1026 officers including Rear-Admiral Hood - only 6 crew members survive
Second largest crowd in Polo Grounds history (61,756) sees Brooklyn Dodgers beat NY Giants, 10-3, endingCarl Hubbell's record consecutive-game winning streak at 24
Winston Churchillflies to Paris to meet with French MarshalPhilippe Pétainwho announces he is willing to make a separate peace with Germany
Avant-garde radio play "The City Wears A Slouch Hat" with music byJohn Cageand script byKenneth Patchenbroadcast in Chicago
Canadian-American League Schenectady Blue Jays pitcherTommy Lasordastrikes out 25 Amsterdam Rugmakers (in 15 innings), and knocks in the winning run
Chuck Berry's amusement park, Berryland in St Louis, Missouri opens
US PresidentJohn F. KennedyvisitsCharles de Gaullein Paris
Movie starJames Stewartretires from the US Air Force after 27 years of service
"Gitarzan" byRay Stevenspeaks at #8
John LennonandYoko Onorecord "Give Peace a Chance" in a Montreal hotel, during their second 'bed-in' for peace; other performers include Tommy Smothers, Petula Clark, Timothy Leary, and Dick Gregory
Stevie Wonderreleases the single "My Cherie Amour" which goes on to become a soul classic
Cathedral of Brasília, designed by architectOscar Niemeyeris dedicated in the Brazilian capital Brasília
Viv Richardshits 189* (170 balls) v England, ODI cricket record
"Harry Potterand the Prisoner of Azkaban", the 3rd film based on the books byJ. K. Rowlingis released in UK cinemas
Rihannareleases her breakthrough album "Good Girl Gone Bad"
Super Rugby Final, Christchurch: Canterbury Crusaders claim their 7th title with a 20-12 win over NSW Waratahs;Dan Carterkicks 4 penalties & a dropped goal for the home team
Usain Boltbreaks the world record in the 100m sprint, with a wind-legal (+1.7m/s) 9.72 seconds
Psy's "Gangnam Style" becomes the first video to reach 2 billion views on YouTube
ComedienneKathy Griffinfired from CNN after photo of her holding bloody head resemblingDonald Trumpby Tyler Shields posted
Kenya's Madaraka Express, a Chinese-built high speed railway from Mombasa to Nairobi is opened by Kenyan PresidentUhuru Kenyatta
Kim KardashianWest meets US PresidentDonald Trumpat the White House to discuss prison reform
Matt LeBlancannounces he is leaving TV show "Top Gear"
ZinédineZidaneannounces his resignation as Real Madrid manager after 3 successive Champions League titles
David Tennantand Michael Sheen star in the TV adaptation of Neil Gaiman andTerry Pratchett's fantasy comedy "Good Omens"
Former "Twilight" starRobert Pattinsonis announced as the new Batman

Battle of Jutland: Largest naval battle of World War I between the British Grand Fleet and the German High Seas Fleet, 8,645 sailors are killed in an inconclusive battle but strategic British victory. German fleet never puts to sea again in WWI.
British Major GeneralBernard Montgomeryleaves Dunkirk
Margaret Beaufort, mother of Henry VII and paternal grandmother of King Henry VIII, founded two Cambridge Colleges, born in Bletsoe Castle, Bedfordshire, England (d. 1509)
Manuel I, King of Portugal (1495-1521) during era of exploration known as "Manuel The Fortunate", born in Alcochete, Portugal (d. 1521)
Érard van der Mark, Prince-Bishop of Liège and Cardinal, born in Sedan, Ardennes (d. 1538)
Alessandro Allori, Italian painter and carpet designer, born in Florence (d. 1607)
Feodor I (Fyodor/Theodore Ivanovich), Tsar of Russia (1584-98), born in Moscow, Russia (d. 1598)
Michał Wiśniowiecki, King of Poland (1669-73), born in Biały Kamień, Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth (d. 1673)
Marin Marais, French composer, born in Paris, France (d. 1728)
Friedrich Erhard Niedt, German composer, born in Jena, Duchy of Saxe-Jena (d. 1708)
Alexander Cruden "Alexander the Corrector", Scottish biblical scholar and eccentric, compiler of a concordance to King James Bible, born in Aberdeen, Scotland (d. 1770)
Richard Edgeworth, Anglo-Irish inventor and educationalist, born in Bath, Somerset (d. 1817)
Pierre-Victurnien Vergniaud, French politician and police leader, born in Limoges, France (d. 1793)
Ludwig Tieck, German writer, born in Berlin (d. 1853)
Johann Georg Baiter, Swiss philologist, born in Zürich, Switzerland (d. 1877)

Cesare Pugni, Italian composer, chiefly of ballet music (Her Majesty's Theatre - London, 1843–50; Imperial Bolshoi Theatre - St. Petersburg, Russia, 1850–70), born in Genoa, Ligurian Republic (d. 1870)
Louise Farrenc (née Dumont), French pianist, composer and only female professor at the Paris Conservatory during the 19th century, born in Paris, France (d. 1875)
Horatio Seymour, American politician (18th Governor of New York), born in Onondaga, New York (d. 1886)
Édouard Deldevez, French violinist and composer, born in Paris, France (d. 1897)
Georg Herwegh, German poet, born in Stuttgart,  Kingdom of Württemberg (d. 1875)
John Albion Andrew, American politician (25th Governor of Massachusetts), born in Windham, Massachusetts (d. 1867)
Hijikata Toshizō, Japanese military leader, born in Hino, Tokyo, Japan (d. 1869)
Ernest Daudet, French journalist and writer (White Terror), born in Nîmes, France (d. 1921)
Stephen Dodson Ramseur, American general and the youngest West Pointer to be Major General, born in Lincolnton, North Carolina (d. 1864)
William Henry Fitzhugh Lee, American Major General (Confederate Army), born in Arlington House, Virginia (d. 1891)
Henry Sidgwick, English philosopher (Wife at University), born in Skipton, Yorkshire, England (d. 1900)
Francisco Moreno, Argentine explorer (Patagonia), and museum founder (he La Plata Museum of Natural History), born in Buenos Aires, Argentina (d. 1919)
Julius Richard Petri, German microbiologist generally credited with inventing the device known as the Petri dish, born in Barmen, Kingdom of Prussia (now part of Wuppertal, Germany) (d. 1921)
Walter Sickert, Danish-English painter (The Miner), born in Munich (d. 1942)
Emily Bissell, American welfare worker and founder of Christmas Seals, born in Wilmington, Delaware (d. 1948)
Mikhail Nesterov, Russian and Soviet symbolist painter (The Vision of the Youth Bartholomew), born in Ufa, Russian Empire (d. 1942)[1][2]
Francis Younghusband, British journalist and explorer (1904 British expedition to Tibet), born in Murree, British India (d. 1942)
Vladimir Rebikov, Russian pianist and composer, born in Krasnoyarsk, Siberia, Russia (d. 1920)
Charles Greeley Abbot, American astronomer (Solar Constant), born in Wilton, New Hampshire (d. 1973)
W. Heath Robinson, English illustrator and cartoonist (Don Quixote), born in Islington, England (d. 1944)
Italo Montemezzi, Italian composer (L'amore dei tre re), born in Vigasio, Italy (d. 1952)
Mark Hambourg, Russian-British composer, born in Boguchar, Russia (d. 1960)
Sándor Graf Festetics, Hungarian nobleman and politician (Minister of War, 1918-19), born in Dég, Fejér county, Hungary (d. 1956)
Lauri Kristian Relander, 2nd President of Finland (1925-31), born in Kurkijoki, Finland (d. 1942)
Alois Hudal, Austrian bishop who helped Nazi war criminals escape trial, born in Graz, Austria-Hungary (d. 1963)
Saint-John Perse [Alexis Leger], French diplomat and poet (Nobel Prize for Literature 1960), born in Pointe-à-Pitre, Guadeloupe (d. 1975)
Jack Holt, American golddigger and actor (Cat People, San Francisco, Brimstone), born in New York City (d. 1951)
Athene Seyler, British actress (Make Mine Mink), born in London, England (d. 1990)
Gregor Strasser, German political activist and NSDAP-Reich organization leader, born in Geisenfeld, Germany (d. 1934)
Konstantin Paustovsky, Russian author (Bespokojnaja Joenostj), born in Moscow, Russian Empire (d. 1968) [NS]
Louis Fourestier, French cellist, composer (Polynice; Patria), conductor (Paris Opera, 1938-65), and pedagogue (Paris Conservatoire, 1945-65), born in Montpellier, France (d. 1976)
Michel Kikoine, Lithuanian-French expressionist painter (Paysage Cezannien), born in Rechytsa, Russian Empire (d. 1968)
Willem Ravelli, Dutch baritone singer (St Matthew Passion), born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1980)
Janet Sobel [Jennie Olechovsky], Ukrainian-American abstract expressionist and drip-paint pioneering artist (Milky Way), born in Katerynoslav, Russian Empire (now Dnipro, Ukraine) (d. 1968)
Margalo Gillmore, British actress (Skirts Ahoy, High Society), born in London, England (d. 1986)
Johan Brouwer, Dutch writer, Hispanist, and anti-Nazi resistance fighter, born in Delfshaven, Netherlands (d. 1943)
Norman Vincent Peale, American clergyman, broadcaster (The Art Of Living), and author (The Power of Positive Thinking), born in Bowersville, Ohio (d. 1993)
Ilse Lagner, German antiwar writer, born in Breslau (d. 1987)
Leonid Leonov, Russian writer and playwright (Russky Lesson), born in Moscow (d. 1994)
Helma Wolf-Catz, Dutch author (Undercurrent, Coral Reef), born in Nieuw- en Sint Joosland, Netherlands (d. 1979)
Alfredo Antonini, Italian-American Emmy Award-winning conductor (CBS Symphony; Columbia Pan-American Orchestra), and composer (The Great City), born in Alessandria, Kingdom of Ital (d. 1983)
Billy Mayerl, British pianist, music hall songwriter, theatre composer (Marigold), music publisher, and teacher (School of Syncopation), born in London (d. 1959)
Henk Schaer [Hendricus Schaareman], Dutch actor (Major Barbara; Lijmen), born in Delft, Zuid-Holland, Netherlands (d. 1982)
Joe Kelly, American radio and television host (Quiz Kids, 1940-53), born in Crawfordsville, Indiana (d. 1959)
Ralph Wood, British contemporary classical composer, born in London, England (d. 1957)

Otto "Toby" Hardwicke, American jazz alto saxophone player (The Washingtonians, 1923-28; Duke Ellington Orchestra, 1932-46 - "Sophisticated Lady"; "In A Sentimental Mood"), born in Washington, D.C. (d. 1970)
Stan Brenders, Belgian jazz pianist and bandleader, born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1969)
Florence Desmond, English actress and comedian, born in Islington, London (d. 1993)
Hans Schaeuble, Swiss composer, born in Arosa, Switzerland (d. 1988)
Valston Hancock, Australian air force commander (Chief of Air Force Staff 1961-65), born in Perth, Australia (d. 1998)
Dominic "Don" Ameche, American actor (Cocoon, Trading Places), born in Kenosha, Wisconsin (d. 1993)
Gene Gifford American jazz banjoist, guitarist, and arranger (Jean Goldkette; Casa Loma Orchestra), born in Memphis, Tennessee (d. 1970)
Nils Poppe, Swedish actor (the Seventh Seal, Dum-Bom), born in Malmö, Skåne län, Sweden (d. 2000)
Sammy Spear, American big band trumpet player and orchestra leader (The Jackie Gleason Show), born in Brooklyn, NYC (d. 1975)
Thor Thorvaldsen, Norwegian yachtsman (Olympic gold dragon class 1948, 52), born in Bamble, Norway (d. 1987)
Francis Avery Jones, Welsh gastroenterologist, born in Briton Ferry, Carmarthenshire (d. 1998)
Edward Adamson, British artist, 'father of art therapy' and collector, born in Sale, England (d. 1996)
Alfred Deller, British countertenor, born in Margate, England (d. 1979)
Chien-Shiung Wu, Chinese-American experimental physicist (conducted the Wu experiment), born in Liuhe, China (d. 1997)
Dave O'Brien [Fronabarger], American actor and writer (Reefer Madness, Kiss Me Kate, 1st Aid), born in Big Spring, Texas (d. 1969)
Henry M. "Scoop" Jackson, American politician (Sen-D-Wash), born in Everett, Washington (d. 1983)
Martin Schwarzschild, German-American astronomer (book Structure and Evolution of the Stars), born in Potsdam, Germany (d. 1997)
Albert Delvaux, Belgian composer (Symphonia Burlesca), born in Louvain, Belgium (d. 2007)
Jimmy Shirley, American jazz and R&B electric guitarist, and early proponent of the Vibrola vibrato arm ("whammy bar"), born in Union, South Carolina (d. 1989)
Akira Ifukube, Japanese composer, best known for his film scores in the Godzilla films, born in Kushiro Hokkaido, Japan (d. 2006)
Barbara Pepper [Marion], American actress (Foreign Correspondent, Green Acres, Rogue Tavern), born in New York City (d. 1969)
Bert Haanstra, Dutch filmmaker and director (Fanfare; Glass), born in Holten, Netherlands (d. 1997)
John Vivyan, American actor (Mr Lucky, Wrong Man, Imitation of Life), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1983)
Judy Campbell [Judith Mary Gamble], British actress (Convoy, Bonnie Prince Charlie), born in Grantham, England (d. 2004)
(Emmanual Tettey) "E.T." Mensah, Ghanaian saxophonist, known as the "King of Highlife" music (Accra Rhythmic Orchestra; The Tempos), born in Accra, West Africa (d. 1996)
Robie Macauley, American editor (Houghton Mifflin), and novelist (A Secret History of Time to Come), born in Grand Rapids, Michigan (d. 1995)
Edward Bennett Williams, American lawyer/team owner (Washington Redskins, Baltimore Orioles), born in Hartford, Connecticut (d. 1988)
Alida Valli [Baroness Alida von Marckenstein-Frauenberg], Italian actress (The Paradine Case, The Third Man), born in Rome, Italy (d. 2006)
Andrew Grima, Italian-born British jeweller known for his modern designs, born in Rome, Italy (d. 2007)
Arthur Sellings [Arthur Ley], English scientist and sci-fi author (Telepath, Power of X), born in Tunbridge Wells, England (d. 1968)
Howard Reig, American radio and television announcer, born in New York City (d. 2008)
Denholm Elliott, English actor (Alfie, Raiders of the Lost Ark), born in London, England (d. 1992)
Elisabeth, Archduchess of Austria, Princess of Liechtenstein (daughter of Charles I last Emperor of Austria), born in El Pardo Palace, Spain (d. 1993)
Patricia Roberts Harris, American politician (Rep-D),1st African American woman cabinet member, born in Mattoon, Illinois (d. 1985)
Russell Endean, South African cricket wicketkeeper (28 Tests, 3 x 100, HS 162no, 41 dismissals; Transvaal, MCC), born in Johannesburg, South Africa (d. 2003)

(Heinz) "Henry" Lewy, German-American sound engineer and record producer (Joni Mitchell; Leonard Cohen; Neil Young; Crosby, Stills & Nash; Stephen Bishop; Judee Sill; The Monkees), born in Magdeburg, Germany (d. 2006)
Derek Birley, English founding Vice-Chancellor of the University of Ulster and sports writer, born in West Yorkshire (d. 2002)
Martin Peake, 2nd Viscount Ingleby, British peer, businessman and conservative MP for North Leeds (1929-56), born in England (d. 2008)
Probir Sen, Indian cricket wicketkeeper (14 Tests, 31 dismissals; Bengal), born in Comilla, Bangladesh (d. 1970)
(James) "Red" Holloway, American jazz tenor saxophonist, born in Helena, Arkansas (d. 2012)
James Eberle, British admiral (Commander-in-Chief Fleet) and director of Chatham House, born in Bristol, England (d. 2018)[1]
Michael Sandberg, Baron Sandberg, British businessman who was executive chairman of HSBC, born in Surrey, England (d. 2017)
Derek Ufton, English soccer defender (1 cap; Charlton Athletic), manager (Plymouth Argyle) and cricket wicketkeeper (Kent), born in Crayford, England (d. 2021)
Gabriella Kotsis, Hungarian Volleyball HOF coach (Hungary Olympics 1972, 76, 80), born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 2023)
Jacob Lateiner, Cuban-American concert pianist, and professor (Juilliard School, 1966-2010), born in Havana, Cuba (d. 2010)
Pankaj Roy, Indian cricket batsman (45 Tests, 5 x 100, HS 173; WR opening partnership [Vinoo Mankad] 413; Bengal), born in Dhaka, Bangladesh (d. 2001)
Elaine Stewart, American actress and model (The Bad and the Beautiful, Take the High Ground), born in Montclair, New Jersey (d. 2011)
